attributes.stem:
A 67-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of bilateral knee pain. The pain is worse when climbing stairs, rising from sitting, and after prolonged walking. She reports morning stiffness lasting about 20 minutes. She has a medical history of hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia. Her regular medications include amlodipine and atorvastatin. She works as a librarian and is finding it increasingly difficult to remain on her feet during her shifts.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 29 kg/m². Both knees have mild effusions with crepitus on movement. There is tenderness over the medial joint lines bilaterally. Range of motion is slightly restricted in both knees.

The patient asks for your advice on whether she should get knee taping done to help with her symptoms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following statements regarding patellar taping for this patient is most accurate?
options: It is only effective for patellofemoral osteoarthritis, not tibiofemoral disease
It should be applied with the tape pulling laterally to correct medial maltracking
It requires weekly replacement by a physiotherapist
It is contraindicated in patients with joint effusions
It can reduce pain and should be used alongside exercise therapy
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical symptoms and signs of knee osteoarthritis, including pain with weight-bearing activities, morning stiffness, and crepitus. Management of knee osteoarthritis should be multimodal, addressing both symptoms and function.

It can reduce pain and should be used alongside exercise therapy is the most accurate statement. Patellar taping has NHMRC Level 1 evidence for pain relief in knee osteoarthritis. It works by improving patellar alignment and increasing the patellofemoral contact area, which decreases joint stress. Importantly, taping should not be used as a standalone treatment but as an adjunct to core treatments such as exercise and weight management. By reducing pain, taping can facilitate better participation in exercise programs, which are strongly recommended for knee osteoarthritis. Patellar taping should be initially performed by a trained professional such as a physiotherapist, who can also teach the patient proper self-taping techniques for ongoing management.

It should be applied with the tape pulling laterally to correct medial maltracking is incorrect. In knee osteoarthritis, the typical approach is to tape with a medial glide (pulling the tape medially), which tilts the lateral patellar border away from the femur. This unloads the lateral facet of the patella, which is often subject to increased stress in knee osteoarthritis. The taping technique described in the information involves lifting the skin on the medial side towards the patella and pulling the tape medially.

It is contraindicated in patients with joint effusions is incorrect. While this patient has mild effusions, this is not a contraindication for patellar taping. The main precautions for taping relate to skin integrity and allergic reactions to tape, not joint effusions. In fact, taping may help reduce pain in patients with effusions by improving patellar tracking and reducing stress on inflamed tissues.

It requires weekly replacement by a physiotherapist is incorrect. Tape can be left in place for days to weeks, depending on adhesion durability. Additionally, patients can be taught to self-tape by a physiotherapist, enhancing their ability to self-manage their condition. This is particularly important for chronic conditions like osteoarthritis where self-management strategies are crucial.

It is only effective for patellofemoral osteoarthritis, not tibiofemoral disease is incorrect. While patellar taping directly addresses patellofemoral mechanics, it can provide pain relief in patients with tibiofemoral osteoarthritis as well. This patient has medial compartment (tibiofemoral) osteoarthritis, but would still likely benefit from patellar taping as part of a comprehensive management approach. The pain-relieving effects of taping may be due to both biomechanical and neurophysiological mechanisms, including sensory effects that can influence pain perception throughout the knee joint.
learningPoints: Patellar taping is an effective pain-relieving strategy for knee osteoarthritis that can be used to facilitate participation in exercise therapy.
Proper application of patellar tape involves medial glide techniques to unload the lateral facet of the patella.
Patellar taping should be used as an adjunct to core treatments for knee osteoarthritis such as exercise and weight management, not as a standalone intervention.
references: RACGP Handbook of Non-Drug Interventions: Knee taping for osteoarthritis. Accessed at: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/handi/a-z/k/knee-taping-for-osteoarthritis

Hochberg MC, Altman RD, April KT, Guyatt G, McGowan J, Towheed T, Welch V, Wells G, Tugwell P. American College of Rheumatology 2012 Recommendations for the Use of Nonpharmacologic and Pharmacologic Therapies in Osteoarthritis of the Hand, Hip and Knee. Arthritis Care & Research 2012 Apr; 64(4): 465-74
Page CJ, Hinman RS, Bennell KL. Review Article: Physiotherapy management of knee osteoarthritis. International Journal of Rheumatic Diseases 2011; 14: 145-151

Warden SJ, Hinman RS, Watson MA Jr, Avin KG, Bialocerkowski AE, Crossley KM. Patellar Taping and Bracing for the Treatment of Chronic Knee Pain: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Arthritis & Rheumatism 2008 Jan 15; 59(1): 78-83
